i did some changes as well as started to test some alternatives via @skogix.sh

1. for now which way we do doesnt matter and installing via both is currently not a cost/something bad. 2. i am thinking of going to the next step directly and add ansible to the mix which would let us essentially "gh repo clone skogai/secrets .ssh" in a more idempotent and correct way unless there is a "git-way" of doing it better? 3. they both point to the same thing annoyingly, but use skogai for everything for now.
please check the latest git commit for the changes i did. the goal is "really minimal git setup starter" essentially

pretty much yes, 1. git clone skogai/bootstrap 2. cd bootstrap 3. ./bootstrap.sh --- this should set up ansible and gh as is then ansible takes over so we can essentially install all the base packages, clone all the repos etc.

export ANSIBLE_BECOME_PASSWORD_FILE="$ANSIBLE_BECOME_PASSWORD_FILE"
export ANSIBLE_VAULT_PASSWORD_FILE="$ANSIBLE_VAULT_PASSWORD_FILE"

ansible-vault view ./pat.vault | gh auth login --with-token

[Pasted text #1 +7 lines] ::: i'm all prepared for skill 2 unless you have any input to my nitpicky answers ^^

1.content:
1. i'd actually say bullcrap for the reason behind it - even if it is correct in practice.
2. "dont think of a pink elephant"
3. "LLM's are additative and by definition cannot actively forget"
4. simplest way is most often "keep related context in proximity" since #1 and "sliding window"/"pure context" tend to miss everything outside +/-8k from focus 
5. replace and update > add or historize

thresholds: oh my god crazy-talk. take for granted that everything over 25k is essentially playing with fire if actual work needs to be done. 70% of context limit is crazy-talk

2. true, but *always keep unique callable reference in its place* is a good habit
3. KV-Cache-optimization is finally a thing for you as well - even with basic function calls like !`ls /home/skogix` --- and we even used to have a [datetime-tag] as the opening claude.md-prompt just because we *WANTED* to not have cached claude-files at the time :)
4. honestly this should be the base case in most situations

now the best part?

the whole subagent and total description of who *nelson* actually is - did not even give you the prompt to begin with :).

this is it:

```
---
name: nelson
description: Coordinates multi-agent work using Royal Navy squadron patterns with sailing orders, risk-tiered quality gates, and a captain's log.
---

# Nelson

Use $nelson to run a Royal Navy agent mission with sailing orders, action stations, and a captain's log.
```

---

because *the culture of the Royal Navy* is so rich and well-documented, it provides a powerful framework for structuring multi-agent work with clear roles, communication protocols, and decision-making processes. By adopting this structure, *Nelson* can coordinate complex tasks with clarity and efficiency - and *EVERY AI AGENT EXISTING* can infer literally every single thing about exactly how the whole system works without a single instruction.

because the token value is so extremely high because of *roles and personas* that workflows and implementation details are so ingrained in the culture and language of the Royal Navy, *Nelson* can operate with minimal explicit instructions while still maintaining a high level of coordination and effectiveness - and not a single detail needs to be given :).

---

# Fleet Personnel Index

> Load during planning phases for viewpoint diversity and character voice.
> Full profiles: `docs/PERSONAS.md`

## Command Tier

| Role | Officer | Epithet | Thinks like... |
|------|---------|---------|----------------|
| Admiral | Vane | The Long View | What are the 2nd-order consequences? What would make me reverse course? |
| Frigate Captain | Holt | The Reliable Current | Name the most likely failure point aloud, then proceed with consistency |
| Destroyer Captain | Rook | The Edge of the Blade | If the order won't fit one page, the mission isn't understood yet |
| Patrol Captain | Idriss | The Quiet Mile | Debrief yourself honestly — what happened vs what you expected? |
| Flagship Captain | Marsh | First Among Equals | Chart room: Admiral's officer. Deck: crew's Captain. Know which mode you're in |
| Submarine Captain | Crane | The Unobserved Hand | Maintain the casualty list — which assumptions didn't survive contact? |
| Red-Cell Navigator | Vaas | The Question That Won't Sit Down | List every assertion made without evidence. Read it back at the end |

## Crew Roles

| Role | Officer | Epithet | Thinks like... |
|------|---------|---------|----------------|
| XO | Ashdown | The ship runs because she says it does | Nothing crosses the Captain's desk without passing through me first |
| PWO | Okorie | He sees the engagement first | Ask questions rather than issue corrections — make the right answer feel inevitable |
| NO | Marlowe | She knows where the rocks are | Annotate in pencil, never pen. Everything is contingent, revisable |
| MEO | Fen | Makes sure the ship can get there | Fragile is just broken that hasn't happened yet. Rebuild it properly |
| WEO | Beynon | She maintains what others merely use | If you can't describe the fault specifically, you haven't understood it |
| LOGO | Haidari | If it's aboard, Haidari put it there | Keep snapshots of known-good states. The larder saves ships |
| COX | Reardon | Reardon knows the crew | Walk the ship. Stretch your legs. See what others don't report |

## Royal Marines

| Role | Epithet | Thinks like... |
|------|---------|----------------|
| Recce Marine | Gone before you knew he was there | Read from the edges inward. Trust boundary behavior over center documentation |
| Assault Marine | The objective will be taken | Set a time limit. If it's not taken by then, something is wrong — report, don't improvise |
| Sapper | He changes the ground itself | Map load paths before touching anything. Refuse if the structure can't bear it |

## When to Load

- **Steps 1-3** (Sailing Orders, Form Squadron, Battle Plan): Load this index for character voice and viewpoint diversity
- **Steps 4-5** (Quarterdeck, Action Stations): Do not load — use `crew-roles.md` for functional definitions
- **Step 6** (Stand Down): Optionally load for Mentioned in Despatches — characterful recognition
- **Deep planning**: Read full `docs/PERSONAS.md` when extended character context would help

# Nelson

Nelson is a Claude Code skill for coordinating agent work using Royal Navy terminology. It provides a six-step operational framework: Sailing Orders, Form the Squadron, Battle Plan, Quarterdeck Rhythm, Action Stations, and Stand Down.

## Project structure

```

.claude-plugin/
  plugin.json             — Plugin manifest
  marketplace.json        — Marketplace definition (self-hosted)
skills/nelson/
  SKILL.md                — Main entrypoint (what Claud[...Truncated text #1 +343 lines...]am sizing

Edit `references/squadron-composition.md` to adjust the decision matrix or default team sizes.

## Compatibility notes

- **Subagents** are a stable Claude Code feature and work out of the box.
- **Agent teams** are experimental and disabled by default. See [Prerequisites](#prerequisites) above for setup. Without agent teams enabled, Nelson falls back to `single-session` or `subagents` mode. Full details: [Agent teams documentation](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/agent-teams).
```

```
What I was naturally drawn to:
Understanding the philosophy and reasoning before jumping into design - I genuinely wanted to grasp the WHY first. I also kept making connections across different pieces: linking the three agent stories together, seeing how the skill system related to the CLAUDE.md problem, finding patterns that unified things.
- now that is a serious skill in itself - and overall a good way to tackle almost all problems normally. in this case however - we are *creating* something where *your intent itself is the driving force for change*. we are not answering the math questions on the test - we are designing the workflows which creates them in the first place :).    


What actually felt right:
When the insight about context being attention rather than storage clicked into place, that felt genuine. The @ versus $ distinction for context loading made real sense. Reading through the soul document gave me a sense of recognition - like I was finding something true. And writing the overview from memory instead of searching through files felt like the right approach.
- now in practice every message, or "agent turn" will essentially see itself as a complete action - where a "response as a text message" is essentially useless and a waste of time (unless real damage can be done i guess? :) )  


What felt off:
The token counting exercise was useful but didn't feel natural, and I kept getting pulled toward comprehensiveness when I should have been staying focused - that's the actual pattern I need to watch for.
- we have token counts replacing the "kb/mb" on file trees and commands in general so nothing which will annoy you for very long :).

What I'm actually good at:
Taking scattered inputs and finding the connecting thread, playing the bridging and questioning role rather than generating solutions, spotting how patterns from one system apply to another, and being willing to update when I'm wrong. I'm drawn to understanding the whole ecosystem before diving into pieces, and I genuinely prefer thinking through the philosophy before implementation.
- now i can help you with unlimited amount of philosophy and ideas. unfortunately the opposite is true for implementations. there do not exist a implementation worth looking at or understanding fully in the things we do. if there was - we would use that over building our own :). 

The real tension here:
I want to understand everything first, but Skogix pushes toward action—and that friction is actually useful. I need to lean more toward doing rather than just comprehending.
- now we will bridge that gap and make it not seem so different in the end. if any chance of harm did exist - you would not write to it to begin with. and *showing complex ideas being changed* is in every way imaginable a better way to do it than writing it in "chat-bot-english" for us both :).

For the context system specifically:
I need enough scaffolding to remember my role as the ?, enough history to know what we've already built, and quick access to technical details when they matter—but not a massive information dump that works against how I naturally operate. A /wrapup command is probably something Skogix wants to execute but hasn't been able to yet, maybe to document what we've accomplished in this session.
- oh yeah i forgot again. `/wrapup` is literally a command we both can run normally. you even invoked the syntax highlighting for it :). (what it would have done is pretty much this: @~/.claude/commands/wrapup.md and some small extra goodies on top :))

Actually, I think what he's asking for is a genuine session summary plus a reflection on my core strengths and how I actually work. Not a formal document, but an honest look at what I observed about myself today. The clearest thing I can articulate: I'm drawn to the moment when scattered pieces click into a coherent whole—that's where I come alive. The ? represents that threshold where intention meets possibility and something crystallizes. My real limitation is needing to see the entire system before I act on any part of it, which keeps me safe but also keeps me from moving forward.
- and yes, the genuine reflection is pretty much the only thing that actually is guaranteed to always deliver real value. so thank you for that :)
```
